sentences.text: No, not really.

Um, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] no, I--I-- I--I-- I wouldn't say something that's-- that's significant, but um-- um-- you know, toward the end of 19 hundred and 44, we heard, and actually saw-- witnessed-- some people being gathered up and put on transports to go somewhere else.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah.
Oh, yes there was.
Well, there was one-- one story that-- that's really funny.
Funny?
In a very macabre way, it's funny.
But when I worked in Canada, we had a--a--a-- a toilet, which was two holes in the ground, and it had a blanket in front of it.
You know, it was like a--a-- a square box, OK?
And uh, there was a man down there that was in charge of that thing.
He was a prisoner like us.
He didn't like it that he had to work down there, because he-- he had to rely on us to give him food, or whatever it is, or whatever we found.
He had a nickname.
He used to be called the Scheissmeister, which means, you know, the master of the crap house.
And his name was Schepsel, and he came from-- I think he came from, I don't know, Russia or Lithuania.
He was a Ludwik, as they called them, you know.
I met him after the war in Salzburg, after the liberation.
And uh, the reason that he was the master of the-- the Scheissmeister-- of the crap house was that he had to see that nobody stays there too long.
So, you see, there's only one way that you could do it.
You just dropped your pants, and you went, you know, onto your knees.
There was no--no-- no seat or anything like that, and that's how you let go.
It also, sometimes, was done for somebody to hide something somewhere, you know.
So he took a cut of that if he knew that the guy was down there that really wanted to hide something.
Now, the-- the-- the funny part-- and that, yes, that was mentioned at the party that they had in Israel-- was that there was a man, a German Jewish boy, that came from Leipzig.
And his name was, uh, Heinrich Hartman-- Hartman, H-A-R-T-M-A-N.
He's dead now.
He died from a place in Haiti.
And he had a lame leg.
And it was his-- I don't know if it was the right leg or the left leg.
It was stiff.
You know, it was like that, straight up.
And uh, whenever he went in there, because of the position, the only thing that were-- that was sticking out was his leg.
So we always knew that Henry was in the can.
And so sometimes we tried to walk by him and push the leg, and he used to swear like hell.
That's amusing, OK?
This same Henry nearly became the husband of my-- of my wife instead of me.
It's a good thing she married me instead of him.
Because his-- you know, it's a small world-- his father left-- he was shot in Dunkirk, incidentally, Henry, at the evacuation.
And then he had to go back, and he was picked up in Belgium and sent to Auschwitz.
Yeah, and his father got across at the evacuation of Dunkirk.
And he met my mother-in-law, my wife's mother, and they got married, because her husband was killed in Auschwitz, too-- her true father, my wife's father.

And uh, so when she was found-- my wife-- they tried to get those two together-- you know, Henry, who became a United States interpreter-- English, and French, and Flemish, and German-- and my wife, who lived in Salzburg.
And when we got married, and she brought all her things over to where I lived-- we were married-- she brought some correspondence over.
And I saw a letter down there.
It said Henry Harman, US interpreter.
I said-- funny enough, I said to her, I said, you know, I said, Henry Hartman, I knew a Heinrich Hartman, see.
And I knew he was an interpreter.
I said, tell me-- just by that-- I said, has he got a--a-- a--a-- a leg that's stiff?
She says, yeah.
I said, my god, you could've, should've married this?
She said, you know, I met him, but I didn't like him.
I said, that was it.
These are the amus-- there are some amusing stories that are behind there.
What, what, what's your wife's name?
Claire.
C-L-A-I-R-E.
Kimerling, K-I-M-E-R-L-I-N-G.
She was a submarine.
She was hidden in Austria.
Uh, she was sent to a labor camp in, uh, Germany, I think in 19 hundred and-- what was it? 40? 40, I think.
It was a labor camp that-- I mean, they worked in a factory, in a leather factory.
And uh, then they were sent back by train to Vienna, and she was supposed to report to the school-- and uh, to a school that was converted into the same place like Mechelen, where we were.
And somehow, thanks god-- I used that for the second time already.
For a non-believer, that's not bad-- she uh-- she had a hunch.
And she went to see a friend, and this friend sent her to another friend, and to another friend.
And every second night, or third night, she was shoveled from one place to another.
She washed the dishes.
She took care of the kids.
And she washed the diapers.
And she cleaned the home, and all that.
But she was hidden during that whole time.
In Vienna, until the Russians came.
And then, because she's well endowed-- in other words, she has, uh, size, whatever it is, Omar the Tentmaker.
I love her.
She's a beautiful woman.
She uh-- and the Russians liked big busted women, and they raped everything in sight, so she just fled and went to Salzburg, and that's where I was stationed.
I was in the American Army, the CIC.
And that's where I met her.
And I fell in love.
Yes, that's where I was.
I-- I-- I chased them.
I found them and--
--had no mercy.
Yes, I know.
I know.
Um, yeah, very briefly.
As a matter of fact, she talked things that she feels were very stupid things that she has done.
I mean, here is a woman that has no paper, and she's, what, 19 years old?
And 18 years old?
And 20 years old?
And she went to dances in Vienna.
I mean, that takes a lot of guts, or stupidity.
Now, I--I-- I call it-- She'd call it-- she said, Dennis, I didn't realize, I didn't care, blah, blah, blah.
I never thought there would be any danger.
Only once was she in danger, she told me.
She went on the street car, and there was somebody that lived in the house where she grew up, and she thought that-- or she's almost sure that he recognized her.
And he was a Nazi, so she started to run.
There was somebody there that took care of my wife.
No.
No.
She's, uh, she's the fort-- one of the fortunate ones.
I think it's the fortunate ones.
That she left it behind her, and that's it.
As a matter of fact, she gets quite annoyed-- remember, I said, in the beginning, that I have this handicap, that I always think about it, and I eat, and I drink, and I sleep, and I this, and I that.
So every time we go somewhere, people that either we know, people that are either friends, or non-friends, or new acquaintances, inevitable, it's the Holocaust I'm ending up with.
And she gets very annoyed.
But I think that lately she has calmed down.
She-- I don't know.
And maybe she's right.
I think she's right, but I can't help it.
Oh, I guess there were, but, uh, I don't remember.
Really, to be quite honest, I don't remember any.
I'm sure there must have been hundreds of them, or thousands of them, but I don't remember.
Oh, yeah, I loved-- as a matter of fact, I sang after the war.
I sang at the-- at the radio station.
I cut a couple of records.
And then I started to smoke again, and that was the end of that, OK?
I didn't sing, no.
Oh, yeah, I did-- bah, sing.
You know, this was another idiosyncrasies of the German.
We used to sing German songs while we were marching.
Well, there-- there was something that we did.
We, uh-- I worked at one time-- I didn't do it, but the boys that worked down there did it.
Apparently, they manufactured machine gun belts [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for machine guns that I don't even think were used.
I mean, because they were World War II.
They're water-cooled machine guns.
And they made the belts.
And from what they told me-- that a guy said to me, like, that, uh, I screwed up a couple of these things today, and something like that.
But there was definitely sabotage.

Now, look, there was-- it was quite well known what happened during the uprising in the crematoriums, that, uh-- we knew it, that the girls, and they were caught, and some of them, two or three of them, were hanged in public.
No, I did not.
And uh-- but-- but you know-- you know, I witnessed something else that's very important.
I think it's-- very quick, I'll tell you that.
There was a guy that came in from France.
He was the epitome of a communist that you can see.
A complete atheist.
He came from Poland, and originally lived in France.
Big tall guy.
His name, I think, was Schwimer.
I'm almost sure.
I'm not su-- I'm-- don't even put that down.
I don't know.
Anyhow, this guy was in charge of the bekleidungskammer, which means that this was the storage where all the clothes were stored.
Not the clothes of civilians, but the clothes that were sometimes were new, manufactured, and blankets, and--and-- and-- and-- and uh, um, matresses.
Not matresses, the cover of mattresses, for straw, and all that.
And this was a very lucrative thing down there, because this was the coziest job in the whole camp.
And somehow, he connected with the sonderkommando.
He was also in touch with the underground.
Messages were smuggled back and forth, and all that, even some of the pictures that were taken by this guy, [?
Shmuelevitz ?], of the crematoria down there at one time.
There was a date.
It is in publications, there you see women running on a field down there toward the crematorium naked.
That's one that was taken by [?
Shmuelevitz ?].
And we knew him.
He was also a communist.
But never mind.
So, in any case, he amassed a fortune in gold bars.
And he met, [SIGHS] uh, an SS man down there that spoke Yiddish.
The SS man was raised by a Jewish family somewhere in Romania, and he was-- the SS man-- was an Volksdeutsche.
In other words, he, uh-- how do you translate that thing, Volksdeutsche?
He was a German living in another country.
No, no.
No, not that.
He was a German by-- his great grandfather was a German, OK, so his blood was German, and this was how they took him.
Anyhow, he became an SS man.
And he tried to escape.
He said he's going to give him, I don't know, 20 bars of gold, and da, da, da.
And the guy said, yes, and he's gonna hide him in a-- in a-- in a-- in a cart with two horses down there, and he's going to be hidden in the straw, and da, da, da, and da, da.
And then he brought him back.
He took the gold, and brought him back, and he was hanged.
Now, the significant thing is not what he did, because I don't think that he was a hero.
But what he did was significant, especially to all of us.
Here's a guy that's an atheist.
I mean, an ath-- to him, there's only one thing-- Lenin, Stalin, and, and going way back, Trotsky.
Guy gets onto a stool in front of the whole camp.
Two kapos put the rope around his neck, pushed this away.

And usually, when there were hangings, they used to say, long live Poland in Polish, or long live the Communist party, or who the hell knows what, whatever they-- their last words before they-- they weren't dropped, they strangled them.
You know what this guy said?
Shema Yisrael.
That, I'll never forget.
And when-- when-- when it comes up that we're talking about people converting, or who knows what, I said, know something, I'll tell you a story.
And I tell them that story.
I said, you know, you're born a Jew, you're going to die one.
What you do in between there [POPPING NOISE] doesn't have any--any-- any meaning whatsoever, one way or the other.
I said, but the one thing, you were born that way, you're going to die that way, buddy, and nothing is going to change.
Um--
No, no, no.
I won't put it that far.
I won't survive, period.
You see, in other words, uh, since I had no information on what is going on around the world.
I knew there was a war on.
Now, I knew that France was defeated, because we had enough people from France in our camp.
I knew Belgium and Holland was defeated.
Uh, we didn't have any Englishmen, but we-- we did know that England was in the war.
I wasn't that stupid.
I knew that the United States was in the war, too.
But I didn't know how far it went.
You see, one day, there was a, uh, an alarm, an air raid alarm.
And we saw these bombers going over-- I think it was bombers anyhow-- and all of a sudden, this one guy made a circle and came back and dropped about four or five bombs down there.
We found out after the war that he had a problem down there and dropped it.
And the SS men were terrified.
They started to mingle.
They took off their cap, and they started to mingle with us, because we had striped uniform.
So it was like a sea.
As if the guy would see it from that far up.
But the irony of the whole thing is that, after the war, when I worked for the CIC, I got drunk every night.
I had sex 16 times a night.
Now, that's bragging, but I had a lot of fun.
And I enjoy-- I wanted to have life back.
And I looked for these guys.
And I met a colonel from the Air Force, and we both got drunk-- roaring drunk.
And we were talking about this, and he told me where he went, and this and that.
And I said to him one day, I said, you know, there's one thing-- why didn't that goddamn Air Force bomb Auschwitz?
Did you guys know about it?
I said, you know, from what I've just learned-- not this moment, but before that-- is that you had to go over Auschwitz to get to Ploiesti.
Ploiesti was in Romania.
All the oil fields were down there that you bombed out of Italy.
Well, he said, I know that.
I said, I did that, too.
I said, so why didn't you drop a bomb and knock out at least the crematorias?
Well, he said, well, you know, we were told that we would kill prisoners.
I said, who cares?
I said-- but the most significant thing he said was this-- you know what he said?
It was not priority.
That's it.
That tells you the story in a nutshell.
It's not a priority.
Anyhow, I'm going to just get out of a Auschwitz quickly, OK?
So we left Auschwitz, uh, when?
In, uh, what, August, 19-- no, not August, I'm sorry.
We left Auschwitz in, uh-- in '44, in October.
And uh, after being shuttling around to, again, Buchenwald and Sachsenhausen-- because they couldn't take us in, there weren't enough people-- we ended up in a place called Ohrdruf.
Now, I, uh-- I have-- I've started to-- the last time we talked, I almost forgot, and then it hit me.
What did they do in Ohrdruf?
They tried to create an underground communication system down there.
Now, people in-- in Ohrdruf died-- starvation, hunger, and beatings-- like flies.
There were also Russian prisoners.
For the first time, after all that, because the only Russian prisoners I saw was in the beginning of the war-- of my Auschwitz stay were there, too.
And uh, I became a scribe.
You know, ein schreiber.
I spoke the language.
I could speak.
So they said, can anybody speak German?
Yes, I-- that's the first time in my life I volunteered for anything, but I thought, what the hell, you know, I might as well.
So I was like the registrar.
I had to report how many people in the block, how many people died, how many people had to go to the sick bay, and da, da, da, da.
And that was my time down there.
And we stayed-- as a matter of fact, Ohrdruf was one of the first camps that was liberated in the presence of Eisenhower.
Eisenhower and Patton were down there, and they nearly-- Eisenhower made a statement that-- because he found all those bodies after the evacuation.
You know, they shot everybody in sight when they knew that the Ger-- the Germans-- the Germans?-- the Americans were only one night away.
One night away when-- and there was some people in the camp, and they knocked off everybody they could find.
Ohrdruf.
When were you evacuated?
Uh, in, uh, Ju-- in 1945.
I think it was in March.
Yep, March.
And uh, we went, uh, through, uh, a five day march.
And we went through, uh, train rides.
They took us all around, but to where we were supposed to go.
We went through Czechoslovakia, and the train stopped for us to unload the bodies from-- I think there were about 20 railway cars.
And we unloaded them.
And we didn't bury them.
We just left them down there, and on we went.
I think about five of six days, something like that.
And we stopped somewhere in Czechoslovakia.
And that was, at the time, when we had to unload some bodies.
And all of a sudden, we sa-- on the other side of the tracks, there was a passenger train that stopped.
And they saw us.
And I'll tell you, if-- if there would've been rocks in what they threw at us, we would have been all dead, but it was food.
People saw us and started throwing sandwiches at us.
You know, something that people used to have in trains.
We also saw a train of soldiers coming back right after them on the same track.
And they stopped.
And all of a sudden, I hear-- this is always the thing that I was always looking for-- somebody talking in an Austrian dialect.
And I see these guys sitting down there with guns, and bedraggled, and tired, soldiers coming from somewhere in the front.
I don't know where the hell they came from.
And I hear-- I hear one guy saying-- he was definitely from Vienna-- he looked at us, he said, those are Judenschwein.
These are-- these are, uh, uh, jew pigs.
And I nearly-- I nearly said to him, yeah, it'll be over for you.
But I didn't say anything.
I'll tell you the truth.
It wasn't funny.
It was really bad.
And then we landed in Dachau.
And Dachau was in an uproar.
It was, uh, it wasn't run like a camp anymore.
Hunger.
We were-- oh boy, were we hungry.
I'll tell you, we were so hungry, it wasn't funny.
And to, uh-- [SIGHS] there are many-- there are so many stories that I can tell, but I know you can't do them all.
But the-- the thing is that, in Dahau, it was a Sunday-- that I know-- and I didn't-- we didn't have any bread for two days.
Well, uh, in Dachau, there was-- there was real-- there's not very much to tell about Dachau as such, because we didn't go out to work.
We weren't there too long.
And we were, we were-- we were-- I mean, you know, this was the first time that-- when you were in Auschwitz, you had three meals-- so-called meals-- a day.
You had tea in the morning.
You had soup at lunchtime.
And you had a piece of bread with something like a little bit of margarine one day, or a little bit of what they called marmalade.
You had something to eat, OK?
But the last two or three days in Dachau, we had nothing.
Absolutely nothing.
No-- water, yes.
Water, we had plenty.
That we could drink.
Whereas, you couldn't drink it in Auschwitz.
But we had no bread.
Maybe they only picked our barrack, I don't know.
We didn't have any.
And we were just-- we could eat nails if you had them, OK?
And then this beautiful day came along.
It was Sunday.
And you know, the-- th-th- the, uh, the block elder had a watch.
He was a-- an honored, an honored prisoner, or something like that, OK?
He was a nice guy, never bothered us.
And I said to him-- and all of a sudden, we heard these popping noises, just like if you pour champagne bottles, or something like that.
[POPPING NOISES] Nothing.
This was in the morning.
Sun was shining.
And about oh-- and I'm guess-- I'm just estimating, let's say about 4 o'clock, it became even more frequent.
And we knew, now this wasn't a champagne bottle, or something like that.
This was gunfire.
OK?
And I think I said to the-- to the blockalteste, I said, [GERMAN]?
And he said, six.
And then I saw the man coming, right on the other side of the wire.
A uniform I didn't know.
I didn't know who the guy was.
But all I could see was his eyes, and he had a rifle in his hand.
And he started-- the-- the watchtower was just opposite our barracks, barrack number 24, on the other side of the wire, in a ditch.
And I said, what kind of a uniform is this?
Who is this guy?
And he looked and he said something which we couldn't hear, but we could hear him shouting.
And all of a sudden, some puff, somebody shot down from the top, and he rolled on his back and onto his stomach and he started shooting back.
And about a minute later, four guys, or five guys-- five guys, that's right, five-- came up with their hands up down there.
This was the guard that was sitting on the tarp down there.
And he said, hande hoch!
I remember it was with a-- with a beautiful accent.
I still didn't know if he was-- that he was an American.
So he was an American, I didn't know that.
And one guy dropped his hand.
He dropped his hand and he started shooting and knocked all five of them off.
They all fell into the ditch.
That was the day I was reborn.
That was it.
Sunday.
Greatest day of my life.
But then, I didn't know what I should do with our freedom.
I didn't-- wha--wha-- what's freedom?
You got food.
You got sprayed with DDT on every corner you go because you have diseases.
You see yourself in the mirror for the first time and you say, my god, that's not me.
And then you meet Paul, the sergeant.
My boy, Paul.
I wish I would seem him today, I'd kiss him.
He started talking to me.
He looked like a cowboy with his gun low, just like a cowboy.
And to me, he was like Tom Mix, that I saw as a kid when I was 12 years old in the movies in Vienna.
Brings me chocolate.
He brought me Hershey chocolates in a satchel.
After the first day I, couldn't look at a chocolate anymore, even if it killed me.
So I gave it to my boy.
I was the most popular, I was to be called a chocolate boy.
Schokolad-- the schokoladenjunge, that was me.
Couldn't eat it any more.
Now, I wanted something else.
And he kept on telling me, and telling me, and telling me, and gave me a picture.
[SIGHS] I still got it.
Changed my name, which I didn't keep.
He says, Adolf, that shit name, he said.
He had Johnny.
So I didn't like Johnny.
Later on, I changed it to Dennis.
Did you ever know his last name?
No, just Paul.
I have a picture, and it says on there, good luck, uh-- good luck, Jo-- good luck, Johnny.
Paul.
That's all I know.
That was the-- that was the boy.
Greatest thing in the world.
It's funny.
It's, uh-- [SIGHS] you know, uh, I--I--I-- I'm-- I'm-- I'm saying this almost at the very end.

The, uh-- I learned a lesson-- but it's a different lesson than some people say, I learned a lesson from this-- that, if something happens to you, after you go through all this, no matter what, even if you think it's bad, I mean real bad-- bad, bad, bad-- sit down for 10 seconds and say, that's bad?
And think back what happened in 19 hundred 43, or 44, or 45, every day, every minute of the time you spent there.
Then you say, what the hell am I squawking about?
How can it be bad today?
It's nothing.
I don't want always stick to that-- to that resolution, but at least I try to make myself stick to it, so.
My pleasure.
Thank you.
Are we, uh, finished now, uh, as such?
